description: Meat: animal flesh eaten as food, named plainly by owner ruling. Phi refuses euphemism here; a diet that includes meat should be able to say so. The word covers meat on the table, not the living animal ('nolika') and not any act of violence, for which the language has no vocabulary.
sound_symbolism: Grounded 'ne', dense 'ku' at the center, settled 'ma': weight on the table, nothing decorative about it.
grammatical_notes: Noun: 'mia nekuma nuola.' (I eat meat.) 'miona nekuma pharelo.' (The person cooks meat.)

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Naming meat as what it is keeps the eater aware of what the meal cost. Euphemism is how that awareness gets lost.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Subsistence diets included meat, and pretending otherwise would be nostalgia rather than wisdom. The word is honest about how people fed themselves.
